iterations.strategy: Root cause: the hot-path cost is dominated by scan execution overhead relative to useful output in a single-table query; strategy category D+C applies by replacing fine-grained dynamic morsels with partition-parallel contiguous scan ranges and a fully fused filter+aggregate loop while preserving zone-map block pruning as an inexpensive pre-pass, reducing scheduler/coordination overhead and unnecessary column touches before qualification.

Root cause: work is highly disproportional to output (about 97.6% of rows are rejected) while the current scalar row-at-a-time path still pays heavy branch and column-touch overhead in main_scan; strategy category C applies by switching to a selection-cascade plan that first filters with cheap predicates into compact row-id vectors and only then touches later columns, with late materialization of l_extendedprice and thread-local reduction to minimize unnecessary memory traffic and hot-loop control cost.

Root cause is a storage-level access-path mismatch: prior plans still scanned nearly all 59.9M rows while only ~2.4% survive, so main_scan remained dominant despite loop-shape tuning; strategy category E builds and uses a persistent shipdate postings index to convert the date predicate into direct row-id range lookup, then evaluates discount/quantity only on candidate rows with late l_extendedprice access and thread-local sum reduction.

Root cause is physical-implementation mismatch (Category B): the previous postings-based plan converted date filtering into cache-exceeding random probes over large measure columns, so memory latency dominated main_scan. The revised plan keeps the shipdate postings access path but changes algorithm by sorting candidate row ids before predicate evaluation, turning discount/quantity/extendedprice access into near-sequential sparse scans; this reduces random LLC misses while preserving late materialization and thread-local reduction.

Root cause is a storage-level encoding mismatch (Category E): the hot path is a bandwidth-bound scan over two wide double predicate columns where most rows are rejected. The plan adds persistent scaled-int16 column versions for l_discount and l_quantity, keeps zone-map block pruning, and uses a fused sequential filter+aggregate loop with late l_extendedprice access and thread-local reduction; this reduces per-row scan bytes and branch-path cost while preserving exact predicates and revenue semantics.
